---
title: "README"
author: "Lauren Fritz"
date: "2026-03-02"
output: html_document
---

## Stop Copy-Pasting: Reproducible Scientific Writing in Quarto

This document provides a summary of resources to be used in tandem with the "Stop Copy Pasting: Scientific Writing in Quarto" presentation on March 2, 2026.

### Learning Outcomes

1.  Conceptual understanding: students will be able to explain what reproducible scientific writing means in practice. They will have an understanding of how Quarto integrates code, text, figures, and citations. They will also be able to describe why separating analysis from writing increases error risk, which is what we are trying to avoid by writing in R!

2.  Practical understanding: students will be able to identify the core components of a Quarto document (YAML, code chunks, inline R, bibliography). They will also be able to write a simple inline R expression that dynamically reports a model result. They should understand how Zotero + BibTeX integrate to allow for automated citations in Quarto.

3.  Critical thinking: students will be able to evaluate when using Quarto is advantageous over Word/Google Docs. They will also be able to identify potential challenges in adopting reproducible workflows.

### Topic Choice

I chose this topic because I have been having a fruitful time lately teaching myself how to use Quarto to write R documents. I have also been excited to learn about the Bibtex integration with Quarto and wanted others to be able to explore this fabulous finding as well!

### Key Information

This presentation introduced Quarto as a reproducible workflow for scientific writing in R. By integrating code, results, figures, and citations into a single document, Quarto eliminates copy-paste errors and ensures that analyses automatically update when models or data change. We explored how inline R code can dynamically report statistics directly within text, and how Zotero with BibTeX enables automated citation management. Students practiced building a minimal Quarto document using their own project datasets, creating a reproducible foundation for their final reports.

### In-Class Activity

1.  Create a minimal Quarto document for their final project

2.  Embed one analysis result dynamically in narrative text.

3.  Generate at least one reproducible figure directly in their report.

4.  Explain how this workflow reduces errors in multi-step analyses.

5.  Identify how this could scale to a final paper or thesis.

### What will it look like when students master these learning outcomes?

When students master these learning outcomes, they will:

Write their analysis and narrative in the same Quarto document without separating code from interpretation.

Dynamically report model outputs (e.g., coefficients, means, sample sizes) using inline R rather than typing numbers manually.

Generate figures directly in their report instead of exporting and pasting images.

Integrate citations using a .bib file so references automatically format and update.

Re-run their entire document with a single click and reproduce all results consistently.
